quick_facts.label: Core customer setting
quick_facts.value: The National Classification of Occupations describes a Medical Representative as contacting physicians, hospitals, nursing homes and druggists within an assigned area to introduce company medical products and stimulate sales.

quick_facts.label: Formal skill-qualification context
quick_facts.value: The National Qualifications Register qualification file maps Medical Sales Representative to NSQF Level 4 and states that the occupation does not have a licensing requirement in that qualification framework.

quick_facts.label: Pharmaceutical-promotion compliance
quick_facts.value: For pharmaceutical portfolios, the Department of Pharmaceuticals’ UCPMP 2024 governs promotion-related interactions with registered medical practitioners; pharmaceutical companies are accountable for actions of their medical representatives and other employees, and gifts, monetary benefits and hospitality to doctors and their families are prohibited.

sections.heading: Responsibilities in hospital-facing medical sales
paragraphs: This role adapts the Medical Representative function to institutional healthcare customers. Typical work includes planning calls on hospitals and clinicians in an assigned territory; introducing and explaining the merits of the employer’s medical products; providing authorised literature, catalogues, samples or other promotional material; following up for customer feedback; booking orders and forwarding them for fulfilment; and reporting daily sales activity to the employer.

For pharmaceutical products, product communication and customer interaction must be performed within the employer’s compliance processes and the Uniform Code of Pharmaceuticals Marketing Practices (UCPMP) 2024. The code places accountability on pharmaceutical companies for their representatives’ actions and prohibits gifts, monetary benefits and hospitality for doctors and their family members.
bullets: Plan and conduct hospital, clinic and clinician visits within an assigned territory.
Present product information, respond appropriately to product questions and escalate queries requiring medical-information support.
Follow up on customer feedback, support order flow and submit sales/activity reports.
Use only compliant, employer-authorised promotional practices for pharmaceutical portfolios.

sections.heading: Entry routes
paragraphs: The National Qualifications Register’s Medical Sales Representative qualification lists D.Pharm, a graduate degree in any discipline, or Class 12 preferably with biology subjects as entry routes. It specifically notes that the Class 12 route was being considered for MSME and rural-area employers and that employment through that route would be limited. The qualification file gives a minimum age of 17 years and describes the role as not having a licensing requirement under that framework.

For hospital-focused positions, candidates should treat this qualification as a route into medical sales rather than as a universal hiring rule: employers may set their own product-knowledge, degree, territory-experience and institutional-sales requirements.
bullets: D.Pharm is a listed entry route in the national qualification file.
A graduate degree in any discipline is another listed route.
Class 12 with biology is listed as a more limited route in the qualification file.
Build practical readiness through product learning, supervised field exposure and the employer’s compliance training.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The national qualification file identifies product promotion, in-clinic detailing, objection handling, sales reporting, after-sales service, support for pharmacovigilance and use of digital tools as relevant work elements. It also identifies critical thinking, problem solving and decision-making as professional skills, and states that communication is essential because the representative acts for the employer organisation.

For hospital-facing work, these translate into the ability to explain product information accurately, build professional relationships with healthcare stakeholders, handle questions without exceeding approved claims, document activity carefully and coordinate with internal sales, medical-information and service teams.
bullets: Clear spoken and written communication in English and relevant regional language(s).
Product and therapeutic-area knowledge, with disciplined use of approved promotional information.
Relationship management, networking and professional presentation.
Objection handling, critical thinking, problem solving and decision-making.
Digital presentation, email, spreadsheet and reporting skills.
Ethical conduct and escalation of questions that require medical or technical support.

sections.heading: Workplace and working pattern
paragraphs: This is principally a field-sales role. The national qualification file states that a Medical Sales Representative works in the field on sales and promotion activities in the life-sciences sector, while the National Classification of Occupations explicitly includes calls on hospitals, nursing homes and physicians in a specified area.

The work therefore combines travel across a territory with customer meetings, product-detailing activity, follow-up, reporting and coordination with the employer’s controlling office or sales manager. The qualification file describes the representative as working under supervision of a Therapeutics Business Manager or Area Sales Manager.
bullets: Field-based work across an assigned geographic territory.
Hospital, clinic, nursing-home and clinician interactions alongside distributor or pharmacy coordination where relevant.
Routine reporting, follow-up and coordination with a line manager.
Work may involve digital detailing and remote promotion in addition to in-person calls.

sections.heading: Progression pathways
paragraphs: The National Qualifications Register file identifies Therapeutics Business Manager (NSQF Level 5) as vertical progression from Medical Sales Representative. It lists Business Development Executive—Life Sciences (Institutional Sales) and Executive—Telesales for pharma, biologics, medical devices and other health services as lateral options.

The same file gives an illustrative pathway in which, after three to four years of industry work as a Medical Sales Representative following Level 4 certification, a candidate may qualify for Therapeutics Business Manager at Level 5. This is a qualification-pathway example, not a guarantee of promotion; actual progression depends on employer structure, performance, product segment and vacancies.
bullets: Medical Sales Representative / hospital-facing representative.
Therapeutics Business Manager or area-management track.
Business Development Executive—Life Sciences, including institutional sales.
Telesales or other commercial roles across pharma, biologics, devices and health services.
